mainTopic: האומר פרעתי לאחר צא תן לו · החייב שבועה ואמר נשבעתי · המוצא שטר חוב בשוק
overview: (1) The Gemara discusses whether a borrower who claims repayment after being told to pay is believed, and Rav Zevid's ruling that he is believed in all cases, followed by a distinction regarding becoming a suspected liar (כפרן) if witnesses contradict him. (2) Rabbi Yochanan rules that one who claims to have taken an oath after witnesses contradict him becomes a suspected liar regarding that oath, a view clarified by Rabbi Avahu and Rabbi Avin to apply specifically to oaths mandated by the court. (3) The Gemara analyzes Rabbi Yochanan's ruling that a found loan document with a date and 'henpek' should be returned, debating the risks of backdating and repayment on the same day, and Rav Kahana's explanation regarding the lender's admission. (4) Rabbi Yochanan rules that one cannot claim against a court's decision, likening it to holding a document; Abaye challenges this regarding a woman collecting her ketubah without a document, leading to a discussion on the validity of the get as a ketubah in places where ketubot are not written. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.topic: האומר פרעתי לאחר צא תן לו
sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara discusses whether a borrower who claims repayment after being told to pay is believed, and Rav Zevid's ruling that he is believed in all cases, followed by a distinction regarding becoming a suspected liar (כפרן) if witnesses contradict him.

Rabbi Yochanan rules that one who claims to have taken an oath after witnesses contradict him becomes a suspected liar regarding that oath, a view clarified by Rabbi Avahu and Rabbi Avin to apply specifically to oaths mandated by the court.
sugyaBoundaries.openingFormula: אמר רבא בר בר חנא אמר רבי יוחנן

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara analyzes Rabbi Yochanan's ruling that a found loan document with a date and 'henpek' should be returned, debating the risks of backdating and repayment on the same day, and Rav Kahana's explanation regarding the lender's admission.
sugyaBoundaries.openingFormula: אמר רבי אסי אמר רבי יוחנן

sugyaBoundaries.gist: Rabbi Yochanan rules that one cannot claim against a court's decision, likening it to holding a document; Abaye challenges this regarding a woman collecting her ketubah without a document, leading to a discussion on the validity of the get as a ketubah in places where ketubot are not written. — discussion continues into next amud.
sugyaBoundaries.openingFormula: אמר רבי חייא בר אבא אמר רבי יוחנן

steps.title: The Mishnah's distinction between צא תן לו and חייב אתה ליתן לו
steps.stepSummary: The Mishnah establishes that a debtor claiming repayment after being told צא תן לו is believed, but if told only חייב אתה ליתן לו, he is not believed.
steps.whatsHappening: The Mishnah distinguishes between two stages of a court ruling regarding a debtor who claims he already repaid a debt. It rules that the debtor is believed only after the final verdict of צא תן לו.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishnah establishes that before the final verdict, the debtor is suspected of lying to avoid payment. After the final verdict, the court process is complete, so his claim of prior repayment is accepted.

keyTerms.meaning: Go and give it to him (final court verdict)
You are liable to give it to him (preliminary ruling)
Believed (creditable claim)
Not believed (suspected of lying)
They write a writ and give it to him (authorization for seizure)

steps.whatToRemember: The distinction hinges on whether the court has finalized the verdict (צֵא תֵּן לוֹ) or merely stated liability (חַיָּב אַתָּה לִתֵּן לוֹ).
steps.confusionAlert: Note that the Mishnah's ruling on חַיָּב אַתָּה לִתֵּן לוֹ assumes the debtor is suspected of lying because he didn't pay voluntarily before the court ruled.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction determines whether the debtor is believed and whether the court will issue a writ of seizure (כְּתִיבָה וּנְתִינָה) for the creditor.

phrases.english: And he said 'I repaid' – he is credible.

phrases.aramaic: בָּא מַלְוֶה לִכְתּוֹב – אֵין כּוֹתְבִין וְנוֹתְנִין לוֹ.
phrases.english: The creditor came to write – they do not write and give to him.
'You are liable to give to him' and he said 'I repaid' –
He is not credible.

phrases.aramaic: בָּא מַלְוֶה לִכְתּוֹב – כּוֹתְבִין וְנוֹתְנִין לוֹ.
phrases.english: The creditor came to write – they write and give to him.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְאָמַר ״פָּרַעְתִּי״ – נֶאֱמָן. בָּא מַלְוֶה לִכְתּוֹב – אֵין כּוֹתְבִין וְנוֹתְנִין לוֹ. ״חַיָּיב אַתָּה לִיתֵּן לוֹ״ וְאָמַר ״פָּרַעְתִּי״ – אֵינוֹ נֶאֱמָן. בָּא מַלְוֶה לִכְתּוֹב – כּוֹתְבִין וְנוֹתְנִין לוֹ.

rashi.hebrew: ואמר - לאחר זמן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the debtor's claim of repayment (ואמר פרעתי) occurs after the court's ruling has been delivered.

rashi.hebrew: פרעתי - ע"פ ב"ד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the claim of repayment (פרעתי) is made specifically after the court has already adjudicated the case (ע

tosafot.hebrew: חייב אתה ליתן לו - הכא משמע דחייב אתה ליתן לו לא הוי פסק דין גמור כי אם צא ותן לו וכן משמע במרובה (ב"ק דף סט. ושם) וקשה דבפ"ק דסנהדרין (דף ו: ושם) אמרינן ה"ד גמר דין פלוני אתה חייב פלוני אתה זכאי וי"ל דהתם מקרי גמר דין לענין שאסור לבצוע לפי שיודע להיכן הדין נוטה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות notes that while the Mishnah implies חייב אתה ליתן לו is not a final verdict (פסק דין גמור), this contrasts with סנהדרין which defines it as such, and resolves the contradiction by explaining that סנהדרין considers it final only for the purpose of prohibiting the judges from eating before the verdict is announced.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות addresses a conceptual tension between the Mishnah's implication that חייב אתה ליתן לו is not a final verdict and the definition in סנהדרין, resolving it by limiting the scope of 'final verdict' in סנהדרין to the prohibition of eating, whereas here it refers to the completion of the legal process regarding the debtor's credibility.

steps.title: Rav Zevid's ruling in the name of Rav Nachman
steps.stepSummary: Rav Zevid, citing Rav Nachman, rules that the debtor is believed in both cases, and the court does not issue a writ of seizure.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a conflicting ruling from רַב זְבִיד in the name of רַב נַחְמָן, which overrides the Mishnah's distinction.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב זְבִיד's ruling collapses the Mishnah's distinction. According to him, whether the court said צֵא תֵּן לוֹ or חַיָּב אַתָּה לִתֵּן לוֹ, the debtor's claim of repayment is believed, and the court will not issue a writ of seizure.

keyTerms.meaning: Rav Zevid (Amora)
Rav Nachman (Amora)
Both... and... (inclusive phrasing)

steps.whatToRemember: רַב זְבִיד's ruling is a blanket statement of belief for the debtor in both scenarios.
steps.confusionAlert: This directly contradicts the Mishnah's ruling on חַיָּב אַתָּה לִתֵּן לוֹ.
steps.whyThisMatters: This ruling forces the Gemara to find a way to reconcile the Mishnah's distinction with רַב נַחְמָן's broader leniency.

phrases.aramaic: רַב זְבִיד מִשְּׁמֵיהּ דְּרַב נַחְמָן אָמַר:
phrases.english: Rav Zevid says in the name of Rav Nachman:
Between "Go, give to him",
Between "You are liable to give to him",
And he said "I paid" – he is believed.

phrases.aramaic: בָּא מַלְוֶה לִכְתּוֹב – אֵין כּוֹתְבִין וְנוֹתְנִין לוֹ.
phrases.english: The creditor came to write – they do not write and give to him.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַב זְבִיד מִשְּׁמֵיהּ דְּרַב נַחְמָן אָמַר: בֵּין ״צֵא תֵּן לוֹ״, בֵּין ״חַיָּיב אַתָּה לִיתֵּן לוֹ״, וְאָמַר ״פָּרַעְתִּי״ – נֶאֱמָן. בָּא מַלְוֶה לִכְתּוֹב – אֵין כּוֹתְבִין וְנוֹתְנִין לוֹ.

rashi.hebrew: ואמר פרעתי - לאחר זמן ע"פ ב"ד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the debtor's claim of פרעתי is made after the court has already ruled against him.

steps.title: Reconciliation: The distinction applies to becoming a כפרן
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara reconciles the Mishnah and Rav Zevid by stating that the distinction applies to whether the debtor becomes a suspected liar (כפרן) if witnesses contradict his claim.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara resolves the contradiction by shifting the focus from initial belief to the status of the debtor if witnesses later contradict his claim of repayment.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara introduces a new scenario: what if the debtor claims repayment, witnesses testify he did not, and he repeats his claim? The Gemara rules that if the court said צֵא תֵּן לוֹ, the debtor becomes a כַּפְרָן (suspected liar) regarding that money. If the court only said חַיָּב אַתָּה לִתֵּן לוֹ, he does not become a כַּפְרָן.

keyTerms.meaning: Suspected liar (one who denies debts)
He was established as a suspected liar
He was not established as a suspected liar
The witnesses testify that he did not repay

steps.whatToRemember: The Mishnah's distinction is not about initial belief, but about whether the debtor becomes a כַּפְרָן if witnesses contradict him.
steps.confusionAlert: The term כַּפְרָן here refers to a presumptive status of lying regarding that specific debt, not a general status of lying.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction determines whether the debtor can later claim repayment without witnesses, or if he is permanently suspected of lying regarding that debt.

phrases.english: Rather, if there is room to distinguish –
This is how there is room to distinguish:
They said to him, 'Go, give to him,' and he said, 'I repaid,'
and the witnesses testify concerning him that he did not repay,
and he returned and said, 'I repaid' –
he has assumed the presumptive status of a denier regarding that money.
'You are liable to give to him,' and he said, 'I repaid,'
and the witnesses testify concerning him that he did not repay,
and he returned and said, 'I repaid' –
he has not assumed the presumptive status of a denier regarding that money.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא אִי אִיכָּא לְפַלּוֹגֵי – הָכִי הוּא דְּאִיכָּא לְפַלּוֹגֵי: אָמְרוּ לוֹ ״צֵא תֵּן לוֹ״, וְאָמַר ״פָּרַעְתִּי״, וְהָעֵדִים מְעִידִין אוֹתוֹ שֶׁלֹּא פְּרָעוֹ, וְחָזַר וְאָמַר ״פָּרַעְתִּי״ – הוּחְזַק כַּפְרָן לְאוֹתוֹ מָמוֹן. ״חַיָּיב אַתָּה לִיתֵּן לוֹ״, וְאָמַר ״פָּרַעְתִּי״, וְהָעֵדִים מְעִידִין אוֹתוֹ שֶׁלֹּא פָּרַע, וְחָזַר וְאָמַר ״פָּרַעְתִּי״ – לֹא הוּחְזַק כַּפְרָן לְאוֹתוֹ מָמוֹן.

rashi.hebrew: והעדים מעידים אותו שלא פרע - בפנינו תבעו לפרוע לו ע"פ ב"ד ולא פרעו הואיל ובפניהם העיז לעבור ע"פ ב"ד אינו נאמן שוב לומר פרעתיו שלא בעדים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that when witnesses testify the debtor did not repay despite being commanded by the court to do so, his audacity in defying the court's order renders him no longer believed to claim repayment without witnesses.

rashi.hebrew: הוחזק כפרן לאותו ממון - שאין נאמן עוד עליו לומר החזרתי ופרעתי עד שיפרע בעדים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that being designated a כפרן means the debtor loses credibility regarding that specific debt and can no longer claim repayment unless he provides witnesses.

rashi.hebrew: לא הוחזק כפרן - ונשבע שפרעו ואע"פ שלא פרעו מיד כשתבעו בפני עדים ואין זה מעיז ב"ד דכיון דלא פסקו לו פסק גמור נשמט ממנו סבר כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that when the court only declares the debtor obligated without a final ruling, the debtor is not considered a כפרן because he did not defy a definitive court order and can still take an oath to claim repayment.

steps.title: Question: What is the reason for the distinction?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks why the debtor does not become a כפרן in the case of חייב אתה ליתן לו.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara poses a clarifying question about the reasoning behind the distinction made in the previous step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara asks מַאי טַעְמָא? to understand the logic behind why the debtor is not considered a כַּפְרָן when the court only said חַיָּב אַתָּה לִתֵּן לוֹ, even if witnesses contradict him. This sets up the explanation that follows.

keyTerms.meaning: What is the reason?

steps.whatToRemember: The question seeks the underlying rationale for the distinction between the two court rulings.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a שְׁאֵלָה, not a קוֹשְׁיָא, as it seeks understanding rather than attacking a prior statement.
steps.whyThisMatters: Understanding the reason clarifies the legal principle behind the distinction and its application in other cases.

phrases.aramaic: מַאי טַעְמָא? אִשְׁתְּמוֹטֵי הוּא קָא מִשְׁתְּמִיט מִינֵּיהּ,
phrases.english: What is the reason? He is evading himself from him,

phrases.aramaic: סָבַר עַד דִּמְעַיְּינוּ בִּי רַבָּנַן בְּדִינִי.
phrases.english: He thought until we investigate with the Sages in judgment.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַאי טַעְמָא?

steps.title: רבי יוחנן rules that a debtor who retracts after witnesses testify is a כפרן
steps.stepSummary: Rabba bar bar Chanah cites Rabbi Yochanan's ruling that a debtor who denies a debt, is contradicted by witnesses, and then claims repayment is deemed a כפרן regarding that money.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a foundational ruling from רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן regarding the status of a debtor who changes his story after being contradicted by witnesses.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ruling establishes that a person who first denies a debt, is proven wrong by witnesses, and then claims repayment is considered a כַּפְרָן. This means he is suspected of lying and cannot rely on his word or an oath to reclaim the money.

keyTerms.meaning: A suspected liar or denier of debts
He was established/assumed the status of
Money/property

steps.whatToRemember: The status of כַּפְרָן is triggered by the sequence: denial → witness contradiction → claim of repayment.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that the claim of repayment comes AFTER the witnesses testify, which is crucial for the status of כַּפְרָן.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the baseline principle that changing one's story after being caught in a lie damages one's credibility (חֲזָקָה).

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן:
phrases.english: Rabba bar bar Ḥanna says that Rabbi Yoḥanan says:
"A maneh is in your hand," and the other says:
"You have nothing of mine in my hand,"
and the witnesses testify concerning him that he has it,
and he returned and said: "I repaid" –
he assumed the presumptive status of a denier with regard to that money.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן

steps.title: The case of שבתאי בר רבי מרינוס as a practical example
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites the case of Shabbtai bar Rabbi Marinus, who denied a pledge in his ketubah, was contradicted by witnesses, claimed repayment, and was ruled a כפרן by Rabbi Chiya.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara provides a concrete historical case that illustrates the ruling of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן.
steps.deeperAnalysis: שַׁבְּתַאי denied writing a pledge for a cloak in his daughter-in-law's כְּתוּבָּה. Witnesses testified he did write it. He then claimed he paid it. רַבִּי חִיָּיא ruled him a כַּפְרָן regarding that cloak. This serves as a רָאִיָּה (proof/example) for the previous מִימְרָא.

keyTerms.meaning: Marriage contract
A cloak of fine wool
Rabbi Chiya, the judge in the case

steps.whatToRemember: The case mirrors the ruling exactly: denial, witness contradiction, claim of payment, ruling of כַּפְרָן.
steps.confusionAlert: The term אִיצְטְלָא דְּמֵילָתָא refers to a specific type of woolen cloak, but the legal point is about the denial and subsequent claim.
steps.whyThisMatters: It shows the ruling applies to כְּתוּבָּה obligations and pledges, not just simple loans.

phrases.aramaic: כִּי הָא דְּשַׁבְּתַאי בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַבִּי מָרִינוּס
phrases.english: For behold, Shabbtai, son of Rabbi Marinus

phrases.aramaic: כְּתַב לַהּ לְכַלְּתֵיהּ אִיצְטְלָא דְמֵילָתָא בִּכְתוּבְּתַהּ,
phrases.english: wrote for her, for his daughter-in-law, a cloak of fine wool in her marriage contract,
and he accepted upon himself. Her marriage contract was lost.
He said to her: 'These matters never occurred.'

phrases.aramaic: אֲתוֹ סָהֲדֵי וְאָמְרִי: אִין כְּתַב לַהּ.
phrases.english: Witnesses came and said: 'Yes, he wrote for her.'
In the end he said to them: 'I paid it.'

phrases.aramaic: אֲתָא לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי חִיָּיא, אֲמַר לֵיהּ:
phrases.english: He came before Rabbi Ḥiyya, he said to him:
You have assumed the presumptive status of a denier regarding that cloak.

steps.triggerLanguage: כִּי הָא דְּשַׁבְּתַאי בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַבִּי מָרִינוּס

rashi.hebrew: הוחזק כפרן לאותה אצטלא - עד שיפרע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי חייא ruled שבתאי a כפרן specifically regarding that אצטלא, meaning he is legally bound to pay for it.

steps.title: רבי אבין clarifies the ruling applies to oaths mandated by the court
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Avin, citing Rabbi Ela and Rabbi Yochanan, states that one who claims to have taken an oath, is contradicted by witnesses, and repeats the claim is a כפרן regarding that oath.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a parallel ruling from the same chain of sages (רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן) but applied to the context of an oath (שְׁבוּעָה) rather than money.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This מִימְרָא shifts the focus from monetary denial (כַּפְרָן לְמָמוֹן) to oath denial (כַּפְרָן לְשָׁבוּעָה). The structure is identical: claim, witness contradiction, reiteration of claim, resulting in כַּפְרָן status.

keyTerms.meaning: Rabbi Avin, the primary sage in this chain
Rabbi Ela, the intermediate transmitter
A suspected liar regarding that specific oath

steps.whatToRemember: The chain of transmission is רַבִּי אָבִין → רַבִּי אִלְעָא → רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן.
steps.confusionAlert: The text says 'חַיָּב לַחֲבֵרוֹ שָׁבוּעָה', which implies an oath mandated by the court (שְׁבוּעַת הֶסֵּט or similar), but the exact nature is clarified in the next step.
steps.whyThisMatters: It extends the principle of כַּפְרָן to the realm of oaths, raising the question of whether all oaths are treated equally.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבִּי אָבִין אָמַר רַבִּי אִלְעָא
phrases.english: Rabbi Avin says that Rabbi Ela says
Rabbi Yoḥanan says:
He was obligated to his fellow an oath, and he said, 'I have taken an…
and the witnesses testify against him that he did not take an oath,
and he returned and said, 'I have taken an oath' –
he is established as a denier for that oath.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי אָבִין אָמַר רַבִּי אִלְעָא אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן

rashi.hebrew: הוחזק כפרן לאותה שבועה - לומר נשבעתי עד שישבע בפנינו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the person is deemed a כפרן לשבועה, meaning he is not believed when he claims he already swore, and must swear again in the presence of the court.

steps.title: רבי אבהו clarifies the distinction between court-mandated and voluntary oaths
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Abbahu clarifies that Rabbi Avin's ruling applies only to oaths mandated by the court; if one voluntarily obligated himself to an oath, he is believed when he claims he took it.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presents רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ's clarification of רַבִּי אָבִין's statement, distinguishing between two types of oaths.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ resolves a potential ambiguity in רַבִּי אָבִין's statement. He explains that the כַּפְרָן status only applies to שְׁבוּעַת בֵּית דִּין (court-mandated oaths). For voluntary oaths (חִיֵּיב עַצְמוֹ שָׁבוּעָה), the person is believed because people often speak casually about taking oaths and then forget or change their minds (עָבֵיד אִנְשָׁא דְּמִקְרֵי וְאָמַר).

keyTerms.meaning: Rabbi Abbahu, who clarifies the ruling
The court
One who voluntarily obligated himself to an oath
A person is prone to say incidentally and then forget/change his mind

steps.whatToRemember: The distinction is between שְׁבוּעַת בֵּית דִּין (strict) and חִיֵּיב עַצְמוֹ שָׁבוּעָה (lenient).
steps.confusionAlert: The phrase 'עָבֵיד אִנְשָׁא דְּמִקְרֵי וְאָמַר' explains why voluntary oaths are treated leniently: casual speech doesn't create a binding legal status that makes one a liar if contradicted.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction is crucial for understanding when a person's credibility is damaged regarding oaths.

phrases.aramaic: אַמְרוּהָ קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אֲבָהוּ, אֲמַר לְהוּ:
phrases.english: They said it before Rabbi Abbahu, he said to them:

phrases.aramaic: מִסְתַּבְּרָא מִלְּתָא דְּרַבִּי אָבִין שֶׁנִּתְחַיֵּיב שְׁבוּעָה בְּבֵית דִּין,
phrases.english: It is reasonable, the matter of Rabbi Avin, that one who was obligated an oath in court,
but one who obligated himself an oath –
[he is believed]. A person does that he says and says.
phrases.notes: Refers to a person casually making an oath and then retracting.

phrases.aramaic: אַהְדְּרוּהָ קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אָבִין, אֲמַר לְהוּ:
phrases.english: They returned it before Rabbi Avin, he said to them:
I also in court I say.

steps.triggerLanguage: אַמְרוּהָ קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אֲבָהוּ

rashi.hebrew: כשנתחייב שבועה בב"ד - דכיון דתבעו בעדים לקיים דברי ב"ד ולא אבה אינו נאמן לומר עוד קיימתי אחרי כן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that when a person is obligated by בית דין to take an oath and refuses despite witnesses, he cannot later claim he still intends to fulfill it, because his refusal constitutes a formal denial.

rashi.hebrew: אבל חייב עצמו שבועה - שאמר לו אשבע לך ותבעו בעדים ולא אבה ואחר כך אמר נשבעתי נאמן ואע"ג דקמי עדים דחייה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that for a voluntary oath, even if the person initially refused before witnesses, he is believed when he later claims he took the oath, because his initial refusal was merely a delay tactic rather than a formal denial.

rashi.hebrew: עביד איניש דמקרי ואמר - לא אעשה מה שלא חייבוני ב"ד אלא אני בעצמי ואין זו סרבנות וחרטה אלא דחייה בעלמא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that עביד איניש דמקרי ואמר means a person might casually say they will take an oath without intending to actually do so, so their initial refusal isn't considered a formal denial but just a delay.

steps.title: The final ruling explicitly includes בית דין
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara concludes by restating Rabbi Avin's ruling with the explicit inclusion of 'בית דין', confirming the final halachic position.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya concludes with a restatement of the ruling that incorporates the clarification from רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ and the confirmation from רַבִּי אָבִין. This is a setup/transition line marking the end of the discussion.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The phrase אִיתְּמַר נָמֵי introduces the final formulation. It explicitly states that the oath must be taken in בית דין. This resolves the previous ambiguity by anchoring the ruling to a court-mandated oath.

keyTerms.meaning: It was also stated
By the court

steps.whatToRemember: The final ruling explicitly mentions בית דין, closing the discussion with a clear halachic statement.
steps.confusionAlert: This is not a new ruling but a restatement that incorporates the previous clarification.
steps.whyThisMatters: It provides the final, unambiguous halachic formulation for the sugya.

phrases.aramaic: אִיתְּמַר נָמֵי, אָמַר רַבִּי אָבִין אָמַר רַבִּי אִלְעָא
phrases.english: It was also stated, Rabbi Avin says, Rabbi Ela says
Rabbi Yoḥanan says:

phrases.aramaic: הָיָה חַיָּיב לַחֲבֵירוֹ שְׁבוּעָה בְּבֵית דִּין
phrases.english: He was obligated to his fellow an oath in court
And he said 'I swore' and the witnesses testify against him that not
He swore, and he returned and said 'I swore' –
He was established as a denier for that oath.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיתְּמַר נָמֵי

steps.title: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling on returning a found loan document
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Asi transmits Rabbi Yochanan's ruling that a found loan document with a ratification (הנפק) and a date written on the same day should be returned to the owner.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a freestanding ruling from רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן regarding the status of a found promissory note. This is a setup/transition line introducing a new topic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ruling establishes that if a note has a ratification (הַנְפֵּק) and the date of the loan is written on the same day, it is returned. This implies that the note is presumed valid and not yet repaid, despite the potential risks of backdating or same-day repayment.

keyTerms.meaning: Ratification of a loan document by the court.
Loan documents / promissory notes.
He must return it.

steps.whatToRemember: The key elements are the ratification (הַנְפֵּק) and the date being on the same day (בְּיוֹם).
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'הַנְפֵּק' refers to the ratification of the note by the court, confirming the loan actually took place.
steps.whyThisMatters: This ruling sets the stage for the Gemara's analysis of why we are not concerned about the note being invalid or already repaid.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבִּי אַסִּי אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן:
phrases.english: Rabbi Asi said, Rabbi Yoḥanan said:
One who finds a promissory note in the marketplace,
and written in it is a ratification,

phrases.aramaic: וְכָתוּב בּוֹ זְמַנּוֹ בּוֹ בַּיּוֹם – יַחְזִירוֹ לַבְּעָלִים.
phrases.english: and written in it is its date on that day – he returns it to the owners.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי אַסִּי אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: הַמוֹצֵא שִׁטְרֵי חוֹב בַּשּׁוּק, וְכָתוּב בּוֹ הֶנְפֵּק, וְכָתוּב בּוֹ זְמַנּוֹ בּוֹ בַיּוֹם – יַחְזִירוֹ לַבַּעֲלִים.

rashi.hebrew: וכתוב בו זמנו בו ביום - שביום שנמצא נכתב:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase וכתוב בו זמנו בו ביום means the loan document was written on the very day it was found.

steps.title: Explanation of why we are not concerned about the note being invalid or repaid
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that the ratification (הנפק) eliminates the concern that the note was written but the loan never took place, and we are not concerned about repayment on the same day.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara provides the reasoning behind רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling, addressing two potential objections. This is a setup/transition line explaining the logic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The first objection is that the note might be invalid because the debtor wrote it but never borrowed (כָּתַב לִלְוֹת וְלֹא לָוָה). The ratification (הַנְפֵּק) proves the loan occurred. The second objection is that the debt might have been repaid (פְּרָעוֹן). The Gemara dismisses this because we do not worry about repayment on the same day the loan was taken (לִפְרִיעָה בַּת יוֹמָא לָא חָיְשִׁינַן).

keyTerms.meaning: Wrote a note to borrow but did not actually borrow.
Repayment of the debt.
We do not worry about repayment on the same day.

steps.whatToRemember: The ratification (הַנְפֵּק) confirms the loan took place, and same-day repayment is considered unlikely.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'לִפְרִיעָה בַּת יוֹמָא לָא חָיְשִׁינַן' means we do not worry about repayment on the same day.
steps.whyThisMatters: This explanation clarifies the assumptions behind רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling and sets up the challenge from רַבִּי זֵירָא.

phrases.aramaic: אִי מִשּׁוּם כָּתַב לִלְוֹת וְלֹא לָוָה –
phrases.english: If because of 'wrote to borrow but did not borrow' —
This is written in it 'the ratification'.
If because of repayment —
For repayment on the same day we are not concerned.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִי מִשּׁוּם כָּתַב לִלְוֹת וְלֹא לָוָה – הָא כָּתוּב בּוֹ הֶנְפֵּק. אִי מִשּׁוּם פְּרָעוֹן – לִפְרִיעָה בַּת יוֹמָא לָא חָיְשִׁינַן.

steps.title: Challenge from רַבִּי זֵירָא citing a contradictory statement
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Zeira challenges Rabbi Asi's transmission of Rabbi Yochanan's ruling by citing another statement in Rabbi Yochanan's name that a repaid note cannot be reused because its lien is forgiven.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּי זֵירָא raises a difficulty against the previous explanation by citing a contradictory ruling attributed to the same sage. This is a setup/transition line introducing a challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי זֵירָא asks if רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן really said that we are not concerned about same-day repayment. He cites a statement from רַבִּי אַסִּי in the name of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן that a note that was borrowed and repaid cannot be reused because its lien is already forgiven (שֶׁכְּבָר נִמְחַל שִׁעְבּוּדוֹ). This implies that repayment does happen and has legal consequences, contradicting the assumption that same-day repayment is not a concern.

keyTerms.meaning: A note that was borrowed and repaid.
He cannot borrow again with it.
Because its lien is already forgiven.

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the principle that a repaid note loses its lien (שִׁעְבּוּד) and cannot be reused.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'שִׁעְבּוּד' refers to the lien on the debtor's property that secures the loan.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces the Gemara to reconcile two seemingly contradictory statements attributed to רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַבִּי זֵירָא לְרַבִּי אַסִּי:
phrases.english: Rabbi Zeira said to Rabbi Asi:
Did Rabbi Yoḥanan say so?

phrases.aramaic: הָא אַתְּ הוּא דְּאָמְרַתְּ מִשְּׁמֵיהּ דְּרַבִּי יוֹחָנָן:
phrases.english: Isn't it you who said in the name of Rabbi Yoḥanan:
A loan document in which he borrowed and repaid –
he does not return and borrow with it,
for its lien is already forgiven.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַבִּי זֵירָא לְרַבִּי אַסִּי: מִי אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן הָכִי? הָא אַתְּ הוּא דְּאָמְרַתְּ מִשּׁמֵיהּ דְּרַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: שִׁטְרָא שֶׁלָּוָה בּוֹ וּפְרָעוֹ – אֵינוֹ חוֹזֵר וְלוֶה בּוֹ, שֶׁכְּבָר נִמְחַל שִׁעְבּוּדוֹ.

rashi.hebrew: שכבר נמחל שעבודו - משפרע מלוה ראשונה ועל האחרונה לא נכתב והויא לה מלוה על פה וטורף לקוחות שלא כדין דאין מלוה על פה גובה מן הלקוחות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase שכבר נמחל שעבודו means that after the first loan is repaid, the document cannot be reused for a second loan because it was not written for that second loan, rendering it a מלוה על פה which cannot collect from third-party purchasers.

steps.title: Clarifying question about the timing of the second loan
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks when the second loan took place in the case cited by Rabbi Zeira, considering two possibilities: the next day or another day, or the same day.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara explores the timing of the second loan to understand the contradiction. This is a setup/transition line introducing a clarifying question.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara asks: When did the second loan occur? If it was the next day or another day, why does רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן say the note is invalid because the lien is forgiven? It should be invalid because it is antedated (מוּקְדָם), and antedated notes are invalid. This implies that רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's statement must be referring to a case where the second loan took place on the same day as the first loan, which would mean that same-day repayment does occur.

keyTerms.meaning: When?
The next day or another day.
It would be antedated.
Antedated loan documents are invalid.

steps.whatToRemember: The Gemara is trying to determine if רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's statement about a repaid note refers to same-day repayment or a later repayment.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'מוּקְדָם' refers to a note that is dated earlier than the actual loan.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question helps to clarify the scope of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling and the nature of the contradiction.

phrases.english: When? If we say the next day and the following day,
What need is there that its lien was already forgiven?
Let it exit that it was antedated,
And we learned: Antedated promissory notes –
Are invalid. Rather, is it not on its day?
Evidently, people repay on its day!

steps.triggerLanguage: אֵימָת? אִילֵּימָא לִמְחָר וּלְיוֹמָא חָרָא, מַאי אִרְיָא שֶׁכְּבָר נִמְחַל שִׁעְבּוּדוֹ? תִּפּוֹק לֵיהּ דְּהָוֵה לֵיהּ מוּקְדָם, וּתְנַן: שִׁטְרֵי חוֹב הַמוּקְדָמִין – פְּסוּלִין. אֶלָּא לָאו בְּיוֹמֵיהּ. אַלְמָא פָּרְעִי אִנְשֵׁי בְּיוֹמֵיהּ!

rashi.hebrew: אימת - דפרעיה אימתי וחזר ולוה אימתי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's question of אימת is asking when the repayment occurred and when the subsequent loan was made.

rashi.hebrew: אי נימא למחר - ולא ביום שנכתב:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara's suggestion of למחר means the second loan was made on a day other than the day the document was written.

rashi.hebrew: וליומא חרא - שתי תיבות הן וליום אחריו וקיצור סופרים הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that וליומא חרא is a scribal abbreviation for וליום אחריו, meaning the day after.

rashi.hebrew: תיפוק ליה - דאפילו נכתב על מלוה זו אחרונה וכתוב בו יום מוקדם כזה הוה ליה השטר מוקדם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that תיפוק ליה means the document should be invalid because it is מוקדם, as it was written for the later loan but bears an earlier date.

steps.title: רַבִּי אַסִּי clarifies that same-day repayment is uncommon
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Asi clarifies that he did not say same-day repayment never happens, but that it is uncommon, so we do not worry about it in the case of a found note.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּי אַסִּי responds to the challenge by clarifying the meaning of his earlier statement. This is a setup/transition line introducing a resolution.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי אַסִּי explains that he did not say that people never repay on the same day, but that it is uncommon (לֹא שְׁכִיחִי). Therefore, even though it is possible, we do not worry about it in the case of a found note because the likelihood is low.

keyTerms.term: מִי קָא אָמֵינָא דְּלָא פָּרְעִי כְּלָל
keyTerms.meaning: Did I say that they do not repay at all?
That people do not commonly repay on the same day.

steps.whatToRemember: The resolution is that same-day repayment is rare, so we do not worry about it in the case of a found note.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'לֹא שְׁכִיחִי' means it is uncommon or rare.
steps.whyThisMatters: This clarification resolves the apparent contradiction between the two statements of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: מִי קָא אָמֵינָא דְּלָא פָּרְעִי כְּלָל,
phrases.english: He said to him: Did I say that they do not repay at all?

phrases.aramaic: דְּלָא שְׁכִיחִי אִינָשֵׁי דְּפָרְעִי בְּיוֹמֵיהּ קָא אָמֵינָא.
phrases.english: That people who repay on its day are not found, I said.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: מִי קָא אָמֵינָא דְּלָא פָּרְעִי כְּלָל, דְּלָא שְׁכִיחִי אִנְשֵׁי דְּפָרְעִי בְּיוֹמֵיהּ קָא אָמֵינָא.

rashi.hebrew: דלא שכיחי [אינשי] דפרעי [ביומיה] קאמינא - הלכך לא חיישינן למידי דלא שכיח ואי אתרמי הוצרכו לומר דאין חוזר ולוה בו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that since same-day repayment is uncommon (לא שכיח), we do not worry about it, and if it does occur, the lender must explicitly state that he does not intend to re-borrow with the same money.

steps.title: רַב כָּהֲנָא's alternative explanation: the debtor admits to the debt
steps.stepSummary: Rav Kahana offers an alternative explanation for Rabbi Yochanan's ruling: the case is when the debtor admits to the debt.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב כָּהֲנָא introduces a new perspective on רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling. This is a setup/transition line introducing an alternative view.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב כָּהֲנָא suggests that רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling applies when the debtor admits to the debt (כְּשֶׁחַיָּיב מוֹדֶה). This means that the note is valid because the debtor acknowledges the debt, eliminating the concern that the loan never took place.

keyTerms.meaning: רַב כָּהֲנָא says.
When the debtor admits.

steps.whatToRemember: רַב כָּהֲנָא's explanation focuses on the debtor's admission as the key factor in validating the note.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'חַיָּיב מוֹדֶה' refers to the debtor admitting to the debt.
steps.whyThisMatters: This alternative explanation sets up a new challenge from the Gemara.

phrases.english: Rav Kahana says: When the liable one admits.
If so, what is there to say?

steps.triggerLanguage: רַב כָּהֲנָא אָמַר: כְּשֶׁחַיָּיב מוֹדֶה.

rashi.hebrew: רב כהנא אמר - הא דא"ר יוחנן יחזיר לבעלים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רב כהנא is clarifying the specific case to which רבי יוחנן's ruling that the document should be returned to its owner applies.

rashi.hebrew: כשחייב מודה - שלא פרע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the phrase כשחייב מודה means the debtor admits to the debt because he has not yet paid it.

tosafot.hebrew: רב כהנא אמר כשחייב מודה. תימה א"כ למה נקט הנפק במילתא דר' יוחנן וי"ל דנקטיה משום דיוקא דאי אין כתוב בו ביום אפילו כתוב בו הנפק דודאי לוה לא יחזיר דחיישינן לקנוניא או לפשיטי דספרא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions why the Gemara specifically cites רבי יוחנן's ruling in this context, and answers that it is to derive a precise legal distinction: if the document lacks the phrase 'on that day,' even if it says 'issued,' it should not be returned because we suspect fraud or that it was written by a simple scribe.

steps.title: Explanation that the creditor would not allow the debtor to reuse the note
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that Rabbi Yochanan's ruling teaches that the creditor would not allow the debtor to reuse the note, as he would fear that the Sages would hear about it and cause him to lose the payment.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara provides the reasoning behind רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling. This is a setup/transition line explaining the logic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara explains that the ruling is necessary to teach that the creditor would not allow the debtor to reuse the note. The debtor might claim that he did not repay the debt in order to avoid paying the scribe's fee for a new note. However, the creditor would not allow this because he would fear that the Sages would hear about the reuse of the note and disqualify it, causing him to lose the payment.

keyTerms.meaning: Lest you say.
This one repaid it.

keyTerms.term: מִשּׁוּם דְּקָבָעֵי מֶהְדַּר לְמֵזְפָּא בֵּיהּ זִמְנָא אַחֲרִיתִי
keyTerms.meaning: Because he wants to go back and borrow again.
He is concerned about saving the scribe's fee.
The Sages will hear about it and cause me to lose.

steps.whatToRemember: The creditor's fear of the Sages disqualifying the note is the key factor in preventing the debtor from reusing it.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'סָפְרָא' refers to the scribe who writes the note.
steps.whyThisMatters: This explanation clarifies the purpose of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling and sets up a new challenge from the Gemara.

phrases.english: What is it that you say: This one repaid his debt,
and this one says he did not repay it,

phrases.aramaic: מִשּׁוּם דְּקָבָעֵי מֶהְדַּר לְמֵזְפָא בֵּיהּ זִמְנָא אַחֲרִיתִי,
phrases.english: because he wants to return and borrow from it another time,
and to save the scribe's fee he is concerned,

phrases.aramaic: קָא מַשְׁמַע לַן דְּאִם כֵּן מַלְוֶה
phrases.english: it teaches us that if so, the lender
himself would not leave it.

phrases.aramaic: סָבַר: שָׁמְעִי בֵּי רַבָּנַן וּמַפְסְדִי לִי.
phrases.english: He thinks: I will hear in the Sages and they will cause me to lose.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַהוּ דְּתֵימָא: הַאי מִפְרָע פַּרְעֵיהּ, וְהַאי דְּקָא אָמַר לָא פְּרַעְתֵּיהּ, מִשּׁוּם דְּקָבָעֵי מֶהְדַּר לְמֵזְפָּא בֵּיהּ זִמְנָא אַחֲרִיתִי, וְלִפְשִׁיטֵי דְּסָפְרָא חָיֵּישׁ, קָא מַשְׁמַע לַן דְּאִם כֵּן מַלְוֶה גּוּפֵיהּ לָא שָׁבֵק. סָבַר: שָׁמְעִי בֵּי רַבָּנַן וּמַפְסְדִי לִי.

rashi.hebrew: שמעי בי רבנן - שנמחל שעבודו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that שמעי בי רבנן means the Sages would hear that the debt was forgiven (שעבודו נמחל), which would invalidate the note.

rashi.hebrew: ומפסדי לי - למטרף לקוחות משום דשטרי חוב המוקדמין פסולין דכיון דהאי דאוקמא הכי לית ליה עדיו בחתומיו זכין לו אף זה מן המוקדמין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that מפסדי לי refers to the creditor losing the ability to collect from third-party buyers (לקוחות) because a reused note is considered מוקדמין and thus invalid, lacking proper witnesses at the time of the alleged new debt.

steps.title: Challenge from a Mishna about found notes with a property guarantee
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges Rav Kahana's explanation by citing a Mishna that says found notes with a property guarantee should not be returned, even if the debtor admits to the debt.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against רב כהנא's explanation by citing a contradictory Mishna. This is a setup line introducing a new challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishna rules that found loan documents with a property guarantee must not be returned. The גמרא explains this applies even when the debtor admits the debt, due to concern the note is antedated. This allows the creditor to unlawfully repossess land from purchasers who bought between the note's date and the actual loan.

keyTerms.meaning: One who finds loan documents.
If they include a property guarantee.
He should not return them.
When the debtor admits.

keyTerms.term: כָּתַב לִלְוֹת בְּנִיסָן וְלֹא לָוָה עַד תִּשְׁרֵי
keyTerms.meaning: Wrote a note to borrow in Nisan but did not borrow until Tishrei.

keyTerms.term: לְמִטְרַף לָקוֹחוֹת מִנִּיסָן וְעַד תִּשְׁרֵי שֶׁלֹּא כַּדִּין
keyTerms.meaning: To unlawfully repossess land from purchasers between Nisan and Tishrei.

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the concern that an antedated note could be used to unlawfully repossess land.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'אַחְרָיוּת נְכָסִים' refers to a guarantee that the loan is secured by the debtor's property.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces the גמרא to reconcile רב כהנא's explanation with the Mishna's ruling.

phrases.english: What is the difference from that which we learned:
Found promissory notes,
If they have a property guarantee –
He shall not return them, and we established it, when the debtor admits.

phrases.aramaic: וּמִשּׁוּם שֶׁמָּא כָּתַב לִלְוֹת בְּנִיסָן וְלֹא
phrases.english: And because perhaps he wrote to borrow in Nisan and did not
Borrow until Tishrei,

phrases.aramaic: וְאָתֵי לְמִטְרַף לָקוֹחוֹת מִנִּיסָן וְעַד תִּשְׁרֵי שֶׁלֹּא כַּדִּין.
phrases.english: And comes to repossess from the purchasers from Nisan until Tishrei not according to law.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַאי שְׁנָא מֵהָא דִּתְנַן: מָצָא שִׁטְרֵי חוֹב, אִם יֵשׁ בָּהֶן אַחְרָיוּת נְכָסִים – לֹא יַחֲזִיר, וְאוֹקִימְנָא, כְּשֶׁחַיָּיב מוֹדֶה. וּמִשּׁוּם שֶׁמָּא כָּתַב לִלְוֹת בְּנִיסָן וְלֹא לָוָה עַד תִּשְׁרֵי, וְאָתֵי לְמִטְרַף לָקוֹחוֹת מִנִּיסָן וְעַד תִּשְׁרֵי שֶׁלֹּא כַּדִּין.

steps.title: Challenge that the creditor would not allow the debtor to reuse the note
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the previous explanation by pointing out that the creditor would not allow the debtor to reuse the note, as he would fear that the Sages would hear about it and cause him to lose the payment.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against the previous explanation by pointing out a contradiction. This is a setup line introducing a new challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks why we do not say the creditor would not allow the debtor to reuse the note. The creditor would demand a new note dated properly in Tishrei. He fears the חכמים will hear about the antedated note and disqualify it, causing him to lose payment.

keyTerms.meaning: And we do not say.

keyTerms.term: דְּאִם כֵּן מַלְוֶה גּוּפֵיהּ לָא שָׁבֵק
keyTerms.meaning: That the creditor himself would not allow it.
Write a new note in Tishrei.
Lest the Sages hear about it and cause me to lose.

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the assumption that the creditor would not allow the debtor to reuse an antedated note.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'כְּתוֹב שִׁטְרָךְ אַחֲרִינָא בְּתִשְׁרִי' means 'write a new note in Tishrei'.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces the גמרא to provide a distinction between the two cases.

phrases.aramaic: וְלָא אָמְרִינַן דְּאִם כֵּן מַלְוֶה גּוּפֵיהּ לָא שָׁבֵיק.
phrases.english: And we do not say that if so, the creditor himself would not leave it.

phrases.aramaic: דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ: כְּתוֹב שְׁטָרָא אַחֲרִינָא בְּתִשְׁרִי,
phrases.english: That he said to him: Write another note in Tishrei,
Lest the Sages hear and disqualify it for me!

steps.triggerLanguage: וְלָא אָמְרִינַן דְּאִם כֵּן מַלְוֶה גּוּפֵיהּ לָא שָׁבֵק. דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ: כְּתוֹב שִׁטְרָךְ אַחֲרִינָא בְּתִשְׁרִי, דְּדִלְמָא שָׁמְעִי רַבָּנַן וּמַפְסְדִי לִי!

steps.title: Distinction based on the creditor's benefit from repossessing land
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara distinguishes between the two cases based on whether the creditor benefits from repossessing land from purchasers.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides the final resolution to the challenge. This is a setup line introducing the resolution.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא explains that in the Mishna's case, the creditor benefits from using the antedated note because he can repossess land from purchasers. Therefore, he is satisfied with the note and does not ask for a new one. In contrast, in רבי יוחנן's ruling, the creditor does not benefit from reusing the note because it is dated for the current day. There is no potential for repossessing land from purchasers, so the creditor would not allow the debtor to reuse the note.

keyTerms.meaning: There, because he has a benefit.

keyTerms.term: דְּקָא טָרֵיף לָקוֹחוֹת מִנִּיסָן וְעַד תִּשְׁרֵי
keyTerms.meaning: That he is repossessing land from purchasers between Nisan and Tishrei.

keyTerms.term: מֵינָח נִיחָא לֵיהּ וְלָא אָמַר וְלָא מִידֵּי
keyTerms.meaning: He is satisfied and says nothing.
Here, since he has no benefit.
Ultimately, the note is written for the current day.
What is there for him to repossess from purchasers?
He does not allow a note whose lien is forgiven.

steps.whatToRemember: The distinction is based on whether the creditor benefits from the antedated note.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'רַוְוחָא' refers to the benefit or profit that the creditor gains from repossessing land.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction resolves the contradiction between the two cases and clarifies the reasoning behind רבי יוחנן's ruling.

phrases.aramaic: אָמְרִי: הָתָם, מִשּׁוּם דְּאִית לֵיהּ רַוְוחָא דְּקָא טָרֵיף
phrases.english: They say: There, because he has a benefit that he repossesses
purchased from Nisan until Tishrei,

phrases.aramaic: מֵינָח נִיחָא לֵיהּ וְלָא אָמַר וְלָא מִידֵּי.
phrases.english: it is satisfactory to him and he does not say anything.
Here, since he has no benefit,
that ultimately the date of the document is written now,
what is there – that he repossesses from purchasers?
in a document whose lien is forgiven he does not leave.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמְרִי: הָתָם, מִשּׁוּם דְּאִית לֵיהּ רַוְוחָא דְּקָא טָרֵיף לָקוֹחוֹת מִנִּיסָן וְעַד תִּשְׁרֵי, מֵינָח נִיחָא לֵיהּ וְלָא אָמַר וְלָא מִידֵּי. הָכָא, כֵּיוָן דְּלֵית לֵיהּ רַוְוחָא, דְּסוֹף סוֹף שִׁטְרָא הָאִידָּנָא כְּתִיב, מַאי אִיכָּא – דְּקָטָרֵיף לָקוֹחוֹת? בְּשִׁטְרָא שֶׁנִּמְחַל שִׁעְבּוּדוֹ לָא שָׁבֵק.

rashi.hebrew: מינח ניחא ליה - ברמאות ותלי נפשיה בספיקא ואמר דלמא לא שמעי ואיכא רווחא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the creditor's רווחא in the Mishna's case stems from the possibility of repossessing land from purchasers who might claim they did not hear of the debt, allowing the creditor to rely on the doubt in his favor.

rashi.hebrew: מאי איכא דטריף לקוחות - מה טרפא יש בזה שלא יהא בשטר שיחזור ויכתוב לו והלא שניהם ביום אחד הלכך בשטר שנמחל שעבודו לא שביק:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that in Rabbi Yochanan's case, since the note is dated for the current day, there is no advantage to repossessing from purchasers, so the creditor would not permit the debtor to reuse a note whose security has been released.

steps.title: רבי יוחנן: הטוען לאחר מעשה בית דין בית דין לא אמר כלום
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Yochanan rules that a claim against a court's enactment is invalid, comparing the enactment to holding a document.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a foundational ruling from רבי יוחנן regarding the finality of court enactments. This is a setup line introducing a new principle.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבי יוחנן establishes that once a בית דין has enacted a payment obligation, a defendant cannot simply claim repayment without proof. The rationale is that the מעשה בית דין functions legally like a שטר, which carries a presumption of validity and cannot be overturned by a bare verbal claim.

keyTerms.meaning: One who claims against a court's enactment
Has said nothing (his claim is invalid)
Like one who holds a document in his hand

steps.whatToRemember: The core principle is that מעשה בית דין has the force of a שטר.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that this applies to rabbinic enactments (תקנות חכמים), not necessarily all monetary claims.
steps.whyThisMatters: This principle underpins the collection of many rabbinic obligations, such as the כתובה, where no physical document might exist.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבִּי חִיָּיא בַּר אַבָּא אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן:
phrases.english: Rabbi Ḥiyya bar Abba says that Rabbi Yoḥanan says:
One who claims after a court enactment –
He said nothing. What is the reason?
Every court enactment –
Is like one who holds a document in his hand.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי חִיָּיא בַּר אַבָּא אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: הַטּוֹעֵן אַחַר מַעֲשֵׂה בֵית דִּין – לֹא אָמַר כְּלום.

rashi.hebrew: הטוען אחר מעשה ב"ד - דבר שהוא תנאי ב"ד כגון כתובה ומזון האשה והבנות הטוען ואמר פרעתי שלא בעדים לא אמר כלום:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the ruling applies specifically to obligations established by בית דין, such as כתובה and מזון האשה והבנות, where a claim of repayment without witnesses is invalid.

tosafot.hebrew: הטוען אחר מעשה ב"ד לא אמר כלום - אינו נאמן לומר פרעתי מעשה ב"ד קרי כל דבר שחייב לה אפי' לא כתב כגון מנה ומאתים ומזון האשה והבנות לאחר מותו וא"ת למה כותבין כתובה כיון דבלאו כתובה נמי אין נאמן לומר פרעתי וי"ל משום תוספת אבל מנה ומאתים גבייה בלא כתובה ותוספת נראה דאפי' אית לה סהדי דהתנה לה נאמן לומר פרעתי. ואין ראיה מכלתי' דר' שבתאי דהוה מהימן לומר פרעתי אי לאו דהוחזק כפרן דר' שבתאי ערב היה אבל בעל שמא לא היה נאמן ואין ראיה נמי מדתני לעיל (בבא מציעא דף ז:) מצא כתובה לא יחזיר ומשמע דלא יחזיר משום תוספת שהיה נאמן לומר פרעתי דלגבי מנה ומאתים נמי מפסיד דהא נאמן לומר פרעתי מנה מגו דאי בעי אמר אלמנה נשאתיך אם אין לה עדי הינומא או אם אין לה עדי קדושין נאמן לומר פרעתי הכל במגו דאי בעי אמר אין את אשתי וא"ת דבפרק אלמנה ניזונית (כתובות דף צו. ושם ד"ה יתומים) בעי רבי יוחנן היא אמרה לא נתנו מזונות ויתומים אמרו נתננו על מי להביא ראיה והא אית ליה הכא דלא אמר כלום וי"ל דהתם מיירי במזונות דלשעבר ולא בלהבא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that מעשה בית דין includes any obligation where the defendant is liable, even without a written document, and addresses why a כתובה is still written despite the defendant's inability to claim repayment, attributing it to תוספת.
